question: A point light in a scene is set to quadratic decay with an intensity of 6.0. Compared to linear decay at the same intensity, the quadratic setting will:

answers.A: illuminate nearby surfaces more brightly and fall off faster with distance
answers.B: illuminate distant surfaces more brightly and fall off slower with distance
answers.C: illuminate all surfaces equally since intensity is the same for both settings
answers.D: illuminate nearby surfaces more dimly but maintain brightness over distance

question: Which of the following light types has decay inherently built into its calculation due to the angle between the illuminated point and the light surface?

answers.A: a directional light with diffuse emission enabled
answers.B: a point light with quadratic decay configured
answers.C: an area light represented as a rectangle
answers.D: a volume light set to outward direction

question: A spotlight's penumbra angle is set to -10 degrees. This means the light intensity decrease occurs:

answers.A: outside the cone angle edge, softening the boundary outward
answers.B: inside the cone angle edge, narrowing the lit region inward
answers.C: uniformly from the centre of the cone to the outer edge
answers.D: only at the very centre of the cone in a focused spot

question: Which statement correctly describes the difference between Drop Off and Penumbra Angle on a spotlight?

answers.A: Drop Off changes intensity gradually from the centre outward; Penumbra only changes from its angle to the edge
answers.B: Penumbra changes intensity gradually from the centre outward; Drop Off only changes from its angle to the edge
answers.C: Drop Off defines the cone boundary range in degrees; Penumbra defines the intensity falloff as a scalar
answers.D: Penumbra defines the barn door attenuation range; Drop Off defines the cone boundary in degrees

question: A volume light set to "Inward" direction means that:

answers.A: the centre of the volume is the light source radiating outward like a point light
answers.B: the edge of the volume is the light source and light travels toward the centre
answers.C: the light travels along the volume's local axis similar to a directional light
answers.D: the volume emits light in the opposite direction of its transform orientation

question: A lighter sets a virtual light to 4100 K to replicate a specific real-world source. Which source are they matching?

answers.A: an incandescent tungsten lamp at typical household brightness
answers.B: a standard studio light used for professional photography
answers.C: moonlight illuminating an outdoor night scene
answers.D: average daylight on a clear afternoon

question: A fluorescent lamp has a Colour Rendering Index (CRI) of 70. According to the slides, this lamp would be classified as:

answers.A: below the manufacturer threshold for good colour rendering
answers.B: within the manufacturer range classified as good or very good
answers.C: equivalent to a perfect blackbody source for colour accuracy
answers.D: outside the typical CRI range for fluorescent lamp technology

question: Which statement about light linking is NOT correct?

answers.A: Light linking can improve rendering efficiency by reducing lighting calculations
answers.B: A newly created light source illuminates all surfaces in the scene by default
answers.C: Light linking allows a specific light to illuminate only specific surfaces
answers.D: Light linking changes the colour temperature of linked lights automatically

question: In Image-Based Lighting, the requirement that pixel values be "linearly proportional" to real-world light means that:

answers.A: the dynamic range must be compressed to fit standard 8-bit RGB channels
answers.B: each pixel value must directly correspond to the actual amount of light measured
answers.C: the image must be tone-mapped before it can serve as a light source
answers.D: the pixel values must follow a gamma curve matching the display device

question: All of the following are valid methods for capturing an omni-directional image for IBL EXCEPT:

answers.A: taking images of a mirrored ball placed in the scene
answers.B: using a fish-eye lens covering the full field in as few as two images
answers.C: rendering a cube map from six virtual cameras positioned at a single point
answers.D: using a panoramic camera with a rotating vertical row of image sensors

question: With 16 bits per channel, the number of distinct brightness values available per channel is:

answers.A: 4,096 values derived from 2 raised to the 12th power
answers.B: 32,768 values derived from 2 raised to the 15th power
answers.C: 65,536 values derived from 2 raised to the 16th power
answers.D: 16,777,216 values derived from 2 raised to the 24th power

question: A reflectance field of a human face is captured using hundreds of lights. The primary purpose of this capture is to:

answers.A: generate a high dynamic range environment map of the surrounding scene
answers.B: determine reflectance properties from every angle so the face can be relit under any lighting
answers.C: create a volumetric model of the face geometry for subsurface scattering simulation
answers.D: produce a light probe image that replaces traditional IBL environment spheres

question: The Phong reflection model computes the final shade value for a surface point as:

answers.A: the product of ambient, diffuse, and specular components multiplied together
answers.B: the ambient term plus a summation over all lights of diffuse and specular contributions
answers.C: the maximum of the ambient, diffuse, and specular terms selected per light
answers.D: the weighted average of ambient and diffuse terms plus a separate specular pass

question: In the specular reflection equation S = ks * is * cos^alpha(phi), the angle phi represents:

answers.A: the angle between the surface normal and the incoming light direction
answers.B: the angle between the incoming light direction and the reflected light direction
answers.C: the deviation of the camera position from the ideal mirror reflection direction
answers.D: the angle between the surface tangent and the half-angle bisector vector

question: The Blinn reflection model differs from Phong primarily in that Blinn is better suited for modelling:

answers.A: matte surfaces such as chalk and unfinished plaster
answers.B: metallic surfaces such as brass or polished aluminum
answers.C: glossy plastic surfaces and automotive body panels
answers.D: micro-grooved surfaces such as CDs and brushed metal

question: Which reflection model is specifically designed for surfaces that are directionally dependent, such as hair and brushed metal?

answers.A: Phong-E model with extended roughness and highlight size controls
answers.B: Blinn model using the half-angle vector for specular computation
answers.C: Anisotropic model mimicking properties of micro-grooved surfaces
answers.D: Lambert model combined with a high diffuse reflection coefficient

question: A BRDF has the property of Helmholtz Reciprocity. This means that:

answers.A: the total outgoing energy is constrained to be less than or equal to the incoming energy
answers.B: the function value remains identical when the input and output angles are swapped
answers.C: the reflectance changes proportionally with the wavelength of the incident light
answers.D: the function produces the same result regardless of the surface normal orientation

question: Which statement correctly describes the relationship between BRDF and BSSRDF?

answers.A: BRDF handles light entering and exiting a surface at different points; BSSRDF handles same-point reflection
answers.B: BRDF describes same-point surface reflection; BSSRDF extends this to include subsurface scattering at different exit points
answers.C: BRDF is used exclusively for metallic surfaces; BSSRDF is used exclusively for dielectric surfaces
answers.D: BRDF models wavelength-dependent refraction; BSSRDF models wavelength-independent diffuse transport

question: When rendering human skin using subsurface scattering, a multilayered model uses three layers. Which set of layers is correct?

answers.A: epidermis, dermis, and the bloody dermis with scattering parameters from medical data
answers.B: epidermis, subcutaneous fat, and bone marrow with parameters from optical imaging
answers.C: outer keratin, melanin layer, and collagen base with parameters from spectroscopy
answers.D: cuticle, cortex, and medulla layers with parameters from hair fibre measurements

question: The Kubelka-Munk pigment model for subsurface scattering was originally developed to describe:

answers.A: light scattering through multiple tissue layers of human skin
answers.B: light bouncing off wall paint to determine pigment requirements
answers.C: photon transport through translucent gemstones and crystals
answers.D: subsurface diffusion in highly scattering materials like milk

question: A translucent material with Focus set to 0.0 will scatter translucent light:

answers.A: primarily in the forward direction through the object like a thin leaf
answers.B: in all directions equally with no directional preference
answers.C: only along the surface normal direction with minimal lateral spread
answers.D: backward toward the light source creating a backlit glow effect

question: In 2D texture mapping, if both U and V coordinates are set to 2.0 on an explicit surface, the texture will:

answers.A: display a subsection of the original texture covering one quarter of the image
answers.B: stretch the full texture to cover twice the surface area in each direction
answers.C: repeat the full texture image four times across the surface if repeating is enabled
answers.D: clamp the texture to a single instance and leave the remaining area untextured

question: What is the fundamental difference between bump mapping and normal mapping?

answers.A: Bump mapping replaces the surface normal entirely; normal mapping only perturbs the existing normal
answers.B: Bump mapping perturbs the existing surface normal; normal mapping replaces the normal entirely
answers.C: Bump mapping modifies the actual surface geometry; normal mapping only affects the shading normal
answers.D: Bump mapping uses RGB colour values as normals; normal mapping uses grayscale height values

question: A normal map stores a normal vector of [-1.0, 0.0, 1.0]. When encoded as an 8-bit per component RGB value, this becomes:

answers.A: [0, 128, 255] where the range -1 to +1 maps to 0 to 255
answers.B: [0, 127, 255] where the range -1 to +1 maps to 0 to 255
answers.C: [255, 127, 0] where the components are stored in reverse order
answers.D: [128, 0, 128] where the range -1 to +1 maps to 0 to 128

question: Which of the following is a key disadvantage of bump mapping that displacement mapping overcomes?

answers.A: Bump mapping requires a multi-pass rendering pipeline while displacement uses a single pass
answers.B: Bump mapping cannot produce self-occlusion or correct silhouettes because geometry is unchanged
answers.C: Bump mapping needs pre-generated normal data from high-poly geometry to function properly
answers.D: Bump mapping is incompatible with the Phong reflection model used in most renderers

question: Each mipmap level is generated by taking half the size of the previous level and applying a filter. For a base texture of 64x64, the total number of mipmap levels (including the base) is:

answers.A: 6 levels from 64x64 down to 2x2
answers.B: 7 levels from 64x64 down to 1x1
answers.C: 8 levels from 64x64 down to a single pixel plus one extra
answers.D: 5 levels from 64x64 down to 4x4

question: A skybox works by remaining stationary with respect to the viewer as they move. This imitates the real-world perception that:

answers.A: nearby objects parallax against the background creating depth through motion
answers.B: distant objects like clouds and mountains appear stationary when the viewpoint changes by small distances
answers.C: the environment wraps around the viewer in a spherical projection at close range
answers.D: all background elements shift uniformly in the opposite direction of camera movement

question: Parallax mapping achieves its depth illusion by:

answers.A: perturbing the surface normals based on a height map to change how light reflects
answers.B: displacing actual vertex positions along the surface normal using GPU tessellation
answers.C: displacing texture coordinates based on the view angle and height map value at each point
answers.D: replacing surface normals with stored XYZ vectors encoded as RGB colour values

question: Which statement about displacement mapping is correct?

answers.A: It perturbs the surface normal at each pixel but leaves the geometric surface unchanged
answers.B: It modifies the actual geometric positions of surface points based on a height map or procedural texture
answers.C: It stores pre-computed normal vectors as RGB values to replace the geometric normal entirely
answers.D: It displaces texture coordinates at render time based on the viewing angle in tangent space

question: Procedural textures used in displacement mapping are generally not stored as images because:

answers.A: they require floating-point precision that standard image formats cannot support
answers.B: a 3D texture stored as an image volume would be too large in file size
answers.C: procedural textures must be recalculated every frame for animation purposes
answers.D: they produce random results that differ each time and cannot be saved deterministically

question: A light map applied to an already-textured surface requires a multi-pass algorithm because:

answers.A: the light map must be rendered at a different resolution than the surface texture
answers.B: one pass renders the surface texture and another modulates its intensity with the light map
answers.C: light maps require a separate Z-buffer pass to determine shadow depth correctly
answers.D: each light source in the scene requires its own dedicated rendering pass for accuracy

question: Spherical environment mapping has a known limitation that cubic environment mapping addresses. That limitation is:

answers.A: spherical maps cannot represent the full 360-degree field of view around an object
answers.B: spherical maps have an abrupt point of singularity on the rear of mapped objects
answers.C: spherical maps require six separate texture images instead of a single unified image
answers.D: spherical maps produce higher memory overhead due to their non-uniform pixel distribution

question: A drawback of reflection mapping compared to true ray tracing is that:

answers.A: reflection mapping cannot simulate any form of specular reflection on curved surfaces
answers.B: reflection mapping does only a one-bounce reflection and cannot show self-reflections of the object
answers.C: reflection mapping requires a separate rendering pass for every polygon in the scene
answers.D: reflection mapping is limited to planar surfaces and cannot be applied to curved geometry

question: In OpenPBR, a material with metalness = 1.0 will exhibit all of the following properties EXCEPT:

answers.A: coloured specular reflections that take on the base colour of the material
answers.B: very high specular reflectivity compared to dielectric materials
answers.C: a visible diffuse component contributing to the overall surface appearance
answers.D: almost zero light penetration into the material surface

question: In OpenPBR, roughness at the microfacet scale controls how reflections appear. A roughness value of 0.0 produces:

answers.A: a matte surface appearance with no visible specular highlights
answers.B: a blurry reflection with strongly varied microfacet alignment
answers.C: a mirror-like reflection with perfectly aligned microfacets
answers.D: a glossy reflection with slightly varied microfacet alignment

question: Snell's Law governs the relationship between angles when light passes between two media. According to the slides, this law specifically describes:

answers.A: how light reflects off a surface with the angle of incidence equalling the angle of reflection
answers.B: how the angle of incidence relates to the angle of refraction when crossing media boundaries
answers.C: how light intensity attenuates with the square of the distance from the source
answers.D: how the wavelength of light determines the amount of energy absorbed by a medium

question: The Beer-Lambert Law in OpenPBR's transmission model describes how:

answers.A: different wavelengths of light bend by different amounts when entering a transparent material
answers.B: the refractive index of a material changes as a function of the viewing angle at the surface
answers.C: light absorption depends on both the distance light travels through a medium and the medium's density
answers.D: reflected and transmitted light intensities are balanced according to the Fresnel equations

question: The Abbe number describes the strength of chromatic dispersion in a material. A higher Abbe number indicates:

answers.A: stronger dispersion where different wavelengths separate more dramatically
answers.B: weaker dispersion where the refractive index varies less across wavelengths
answers.C: a higher overall refractive index with stronger total light bending
answers.D: a wider absorption spectrum with more wavelengths being filtered out

question: In OpenPBR, the Fuzz component models materials like velvet and suede. These materials exhibit:

answers.A: sharp specular highlights that appear at the normal incidence viewing angle
answers.B: iridescent colour shifts caused by thin-film interference between surface layers
answers.C: a soft glow near grazing angles caused by tiny surface fibres scattering light
answers.D: a clear transparent coating layer that adds reflective highlights on top of the base

question: Thin Film effects in OpenPBR produce iridescent colours because of:

answers.A: subsurface scattering within a very thin translucent layer that tints the exiting light
answers.B: interference between reflected light waves from the top and bottom of a thin transparent layer
answers.C: chromatic dispersion caused by a high Abbe number splitting white light into components
answers.D: diffraction of light at the edges of micro-grooves aligned on the material surface

question: The Fresnel effect in OpenPBR explains why water observed at a steep glancing angle appears:

answers.A: more transparent because refraction dominates at shallow viewing angles
answers.B: darker because absorption increases with the path length through the medium
answers.C: more reflective because Fresnel equations shift the balance from refraction to reflection
answers.D: more colourful because dispersion increases as the angle of incidence grows

question: In OpenPBR, albedo specifically refers to:

answers.A: the total amount of light a surface reflects including specular highlights and glossy reflections
answers.B: the fraction of incoming light that a surface reflects diffusely, independent of lighting conditions
answers.C: the base colour multiplied by the metalness parameter to determine the overall surface tint
answers.D: the combined emission and reflection properties stored in the material's base colour map

question: According to the slides, which statement about shaders and materials is correct?

answers.A: A shader is the set of parameter values while a material is the algorithm that interprets them
answers.B: A material is the data describing surface properties while a shader is the algorithm interpreting that data
answers.C: A shader and a material are interchangeable terms referring to the same rendering component
answers.D: A material contains only texture maps while a shader contains only mathematical lighting equations

question: The rendering equation by Kajiya (1986) is an approximation of Maxwell's equations for electromagnetics. Which optical phenomena can it NOT simulate?

answers.A: reflection, refraction, and caustic light concentration through transparent objects
answers.B: diffraction, interference, and polarisation of light waves
answers.C: dispersion of light into component wavelengths through a prism
answers.D: ambient occlusion, soft shadows, and indirect diffuse illumination

question: In forward ray tracing, rays are traced from the eye through each pixel into the scene. This approach is preferred over tracing from lights because:

answers.A: light-based rays produce less accurate colour calculations at intersection points
answers.B: many light-based rays never reach the camera, wasting computational resources
answers.C: eye-based rays can simulate refraction while light-based rays are limited to reflection
answers.D: light-based rays cannot interact with transparent or translucent material surfaces

question: When a ray in a ray tracer hits an object, a shadow ray is cast directly to the light source. If this shadow ray intersects another object before reaching the light, the original intersection point is:

answers.A: rendered using only the ambient component of the lighting model
answers.B: determined to be in shadow because the shadow ray was occluded
answers.C: re-traced with an additional bounce to find an alternative light path
answers.D: coloured using the material properties of the occluding object instead

question: In ray tracing, when a ray intersects more than one object, the ray tracer selects the intersection point that is:

answers.A: farthest from the ray origin to ensure all objects contribute to the final colour
answers.B: closest to the ray origin to determine which object is the occluding surface
answers.C: most perpendicular to the surface normal for the best shading accuracy
answers.D: nearest to the light source to maximize the diffuse illumination component

question: Ray tracing makes which of the following traditional rendering steps redundant?

answers.A: texture coordinate interpolation across polygon surfaces during scan conversion
answers.B: hidden surface determination and z-buffer visibility testing for occlusion
answers.C: projection of polygons from world coordinates into the canonical view volume
answers.D: computation of surface normals at vertices for per-pixel shading calculations

question: Each time a reflected ray hits a partially transparent object, the ray tracer spawns both a reflected ray and a refracted ray. This recursive spawning creates:

answers.A: a linked list of rays ordered by intersection distance from the camera
answers.B: a tree of rays where each node is a reflected or refracted sub-ray
answers.C: a stack of rays processed in last-in first-out order for depth priority
answers.D: a queue of rays processed in first-in first-out order for breadth coverage

question: Radiosity rendering tracks only diffuse light transport, while basic ray tracing tracks only specular transport. A consequence of this difference is that:

answers.A: radiosity results are view-dependent and must be recalculated when the camera moves
answers.B: ray tracing results are view-independent and can be used for real-time walkthroughs
answers.C: radiosity results are view-independent and can be pre-processed for real-time traversal
answers.D: ray tracing produces softer shadows while radiosity produces sharper shadow boundaries

question: In the radiosity method, the scene is first divided into flat polygons called patches. The algorithm then computes form factors that describe:

answers.A: the geometric complexity of each patch relative to the total polygon count in the scene
answers.B: the fraction of light leaving one patch that arrives at and illuminates another patch
answers.C: the specular reflection coefficient of each patch based on its material properties
answers.D: the angle between each patch normal and the primary directional light source

question: In photon mapping, after a photon intersects a surface the intersection point, incoming direction, and energy are stored. The new reflection direction is then chosen using:

answers.A: the surface normal vector to compute a perfect mirror reflection direction
answers.B: Snell's Law of refraction to determine the transmitted ray angle
answers.C: the surface's bidirectional reflectance distribution function (BRDF)
answers.D: a fixed set of pre-computed bounce directions stored in a lookup table

question: In photon mapping, the "Russian Roulette" technique determines when a photon stops reflecting by:

answers.A: decreasing the photon's energy at each intersection until it falls below a fixed threshold
answers.B: assigning a probability of continuation at each intersection that decreases with each bounce
answers.C: counting the total number of reflections and terminating after a fixed maximum depth
answers.D: comparing the photon's current wavelength against the material's absorption spectrum

question: A scan-line renderer has a performance advantage over ray tracing for pixels that do not intersect any geometry because:

answers.A: scan-line rendering skips projection calculations for polygons outside the view frustum
answers.B: scan-line rendering requires no computation for pixels without geometry intersection
answers.C: ray tracing cannot detect empty pixels without first casting a ray for each one
answers.D: ray tracing must perform a z-buffer comparison even when no geometry is present

question: Path tracing differs from traditional ray tracing in that path tracing inherently simulates:

answers.A: only specular reflections and refractions through perfectly smooth surfaces
answers.B: depth of field, soft shadows, caustics, motion blur, and indirect lighting without special additions
answers.C: shadow volumes and stencil buffer operations for volumetric shadow calculation
answers.D: form factors between surface patches for view-independent diffuse illumination

question: The random element in path tracing causes rendering noise. This noise is reduced by:

answers.A: increasing the number of light bounces computed per ray path
answers.B: increasing the total number of samples computed per pixel
answers.C: decreasing the resolution of the output image to average more rays
answers.D: decreasing the maximum path depth to simplify the calculation

question: Metropolis Light Transport has an advantage over standard bidirectional path tracing because once a valid light path is found, the algorithm can:

answers.A: cache the path permanently so it never needs to be recalculated for any future frame
answers.B: explore nearby paths by modifying nodes, allowing obscure light paths to be determined more thoroughly
answers.C: convert the path into a form factor for radiosity-style view-independent pre-computation
answers.D: split the path into separate reflection and refraction components processed in parallel

question: Final Gather calculates diffuse irradiance from indirect light but does NOT produce:

answers.A: illumination from surfaces that reflect ambient light in the scene
answers.B: illumination from environment images used as background shading
answers.C: specular highlights on glossy or mirror-like surfaces in the scene
answers.D: illumination from surfaces that emit their own radiance via shaders

question: Final Gather is described as a two-stage process. The correct sequence of these stages is:

answers.A: first render the scene, then distribute FG points to gather residual irradiance
answers.B: first distribute FG points and gather irradiance, then interpolate those values during rendering
answers.C: first emit photons from light sources, then collect them at surface intersection points
answers.D: first trace rays from the camera, then reflect those rays back toward light sources

question: Importons differ from photons in that importons are:

answers.A: emitted from light sources and carry energy values representing radiant flux
answers.B: emitted from the camera and carry importance values representing contribution to the final image
answers.C: stored in a photon map and used to estimate direct illumination at surface points
answers.D: used exclusively for caustic calculations involving refraction through transparent materials

question: Caustics in rendering simulate light that has been concentrated by reflection or refraction. The most common method for generating caustics is:

answers.A: radiosity with high patch subdivision near transparent surfaces
answers.B: photon mapping where photons are traced from lights through refractive and reflective objects
answers.C: ambient occlusion with increased hemispherical ray sampling density
answers.D: final gather with additional irradiance particles placed near glass surfaces

question: In the ambient occlusion algorithm, a surface point sends out a hemispherical spray of rays. If 2 out of 6 rays are occluded, the occlusion ratio is 1/3, and the ambient term for that point becomes:

answers.A: 1/3 of the maximum ambient value, representing the fraction that is blocked
answers.B: 2/3 of the maximum ambient value, representing the fraction that is unblocked
answers.C: zero because any occlusion eliminates the ambient contribution entirely
answers.D: the full ambient value because fewer than half the rays were occluded

question: Ambient occlusion was introduced in 2002 by:

answers.A: Pixar Animation Studios for use in their RenderMan pipeline
answers.B: Industrial Light and Magic as a method for quick grounded realism
answers.C: Mental Images as part of the Mental Ray global illumination system
answers.D: Solid Angle as a feature of the Arnold path tracing renderer

question: A billboard polygon in image-based rendering always orients itself to face the viewer. This technique is called:

answers.A: impostering where complex geometry is replaced by a pre-rendered texture at runtime
answers.B: billboarding where a textured polygon rotates to maintain its orientation toward the camera
answers.C: sprite rendering where a fixed 2D image is scaled based on distance from the viewport
answers.D: decal projection where a texture is projected onto nearby geometry in screen space

question: Which billboarding technique is commonly used for displaying trees by rotating around a fixed world-space axis?

answers.A: screen-aligned billboarding with the camera's up vector
answers.B: world-oriented billboarding with the world's up vector
answers.C: axial billboarding with the tree trunk axis as the fixed rotation axis
answers.D: imposter billboarding with on-the-fly texture generation from the current viewpoint

question: A lens flare effect consists of two components. The halo is caused by:

answers.A: density fluctuations in the lens producing radiating rays from the light point
answers.B: the lens refracting different wavelengths by different amounts, similar to a prism
answers.C: overexposure of the camera sensor creating a uniform bright circle on the image
answers.D: multiple internal reflections between the front and rear lens element surfaces

question: In toon shading, the two-tone (hard shading) approach determines whether a surface point is lit or shadowed by evaluating:

answers.A: the dot product of the surface normal and light vector, checking if the result is below zero
answers.B: the distance from the surface point to the nearest light source against a threshold
answers.C: the specular reflection intensity compared to the diffuse reflection intensity
answers.D: the angle between the surface normal and the camera view direction for silhouette detection

question: In non-photorealistic rendering, all of the following are types of edges used in toon rendering EXCEPT:

answers.A: a boundary edge that is not shared by two polygons, such as a paper edge
answers.B: a crease edge shared by two polygons where the angle between them exceeds a threshold
answers.C: a material edge where two polygons sharing an edge differ in their assigned material
answers.D: a specular edge where the highlight intensity exceeds a luminance threshold value

question: The surface angle silhouetting technique determines silhouette edges by computing the dot product between the view direction and the surface normal. An edge is likely a silhouette when this dot product is:

answers.A: close to positive one, indicating the surface faces directly toward the camera
answers.B: close to negative one, indicating the surface faces directly away from the camera
answers.C: close to zero, indicating the surface is nearly edge-on to the viewing direction
answers.D: exactly equal to the cosine of the cone angle defined for the edge detection

question: Procedural geometry silhouetting renders silhouette edges by first drawing front-faces normally, then:

answers.A: applying an edge detection filter to the z-buffer to find depth discontinuities
answers.B: turning on front-face culling and turning off back-face culling to display only back-faces
answers.C: re-rendering the entire scene with a thicker wireframe overlay in a contrasting colour
answers.D: computing form factors between adjacent polygons to identify orientation boundaries

question: Silhouetting by image processing finds edges by looking for discontinuities in which data?

answers.A: the colour buffer values between adjacent pixels in the rendered frame
answers.B: neighbouring z-buffer depth values and neighbouring surface normal values
answers.C: the texture coordinate gradients across polygon boundaries in UV space
answers.D: the stencil buffer counts that track shadow volume entry and exit crossings

question: Rotoscoping is a technique where animators:

answers.A: capture an actor's motion using sensors and translate it into skeletal animation data
answers.B: sketch over live-action film frame-by-frame to create animated or composited imagery
answers.C: apply non-photorealistic shaders to photorealistic renders to achieve a hand-drawn look
answers.D: generate mattes automatically using chromakey to separate foreground from background

question: In network rendering, the group of machines that render individual frames is called a:

answers.A: cluster, where each machine processes a bucket of a single frame simultaneously
answers.B: farm, where each machine (node) renders complete frames assigned by a master
answers.C: grid, where machines volunteer idle cycles to process portions of the rendering queue
answers.D: pipeline, where each machine handles one specific stage of the rendering process

question: The total render time equation is T_TRT = T_APF x C_RR x T_AL x FPS. In this equation, C_RR represents:

answers.A: the count of rendering cores available across all nodes in the render farm
answers.B: the count of re-renders required per frame due to revisions and corrections
answers.C: the compression ratio applied to each rendered frame for storage optimization
answers.D: the cumulative resolution factor based on the output image dimensions

question: A key limitation of network rendering that distributed rendering overcomes is:

answers.A: network rendering cannot handle scenes with more than a fixed number of texture maps
answers.B: network rendering cannot handle inter-frame dependencies from dynamic simulations like cloth or fluid
answers.C: network rendering produces lower quality results due to lossy frame compression during transfer
answers.D: network rendering requires all slave machines to use identical hardware specifications

question: Distributed rendering differs from network rendering in that distributed rendering:

answers.A: sends complete frames to individual machines and assembles the results afterward
answers.B: breaks each frame into buckets and sends each bucket to a different machine simultaneously
answers.C: uses only GPU-based renderers while network rendering relies on CPU-based computation
answers.D: requires fewer machines but assigns multiple sequential frames to each available node

question: When rendering uncompressed frames for a Blu-Ray resolution output (1080x1920, 3 channels), the approximate size of each raw frame is:

answers.A: approximately 2 million bytes based on the total pixel count alone
answers.B: approximately 6 million bytes based on total pixels multiplied by three colour channels
answers.C: approximately 12 million bytes based on total pixels multiplied by six data components
answers.D: approximately 24 million bytes based on total pixels at four bytes per channel

question: In shadow mapping, the scene is first rendered from the position of the light source to generate a depth buffer. A point in the final render is determined to be in shadow when:

answers.A: its distance from the light equals the stored depth value in the shadow map exactly
answers.B: its distance from the light is greater than the stored depth value in the shadow map
answers.C: its distance from the light is less than the stored depth value in the shadow map
answers.D: its surface normal points away from the light source direction at the sample point

question: Shadow volumes determine whether a point is in shadow by tracking a counter as a ray passes through shadow volume boundaries. The point is in shadow when:

answers.A: the counter equals zero after passing through all front and back faces of shadow volumes
answers.B: the counter is greater than zero after incrementing on front faces and decrementing on back faces
answers.C: the counter is negative after decrementing on front faces and incrementing on back faces
answers.D: the counter equals the total number of shadow-casting polygons visible from the camera

question: A shadow map does NOT need to be re-rendered when:

answers.A: the light source moves to a different position in the scene between frames
answers.B: the camera moves to a new viewpoint while the light and shadow-casting objects remain static
answers.C: the shadow-casting object changes its geometry or position in the scene
answers.D: a new occluding object is added to the scene between the light and the receiver

question: Soft planar shadows can be approximated by sampling an area light with multiple point lights. The final soft shadow is produced by:

answers.A: selecting the darkest shadow image from all the point light samples for maximum contrast
answers.B: averaging the shadow images rendered from each point light sample on the area surface
answers.C: blurring a single hard shadow using a Gaussian filter proportional to the light area
answers.D: tracing additional shadow rays from the penumbra region toward random positions on the light

question: Projection shadows work by rendering a 3D object a second time using a matrix that:

answers.A: scales the object to zero height along the Y axis and translates it to the shadow receiver plane
answers.B: projects the object's vertices onto a plane using the light position and similar triangles
answers.C: rotates the object geometry to align with the light direction and flattens it along that axis
answers.D: creates a z-buffer depth image from the light's viewpoint for later shadow comparison

question: Fog effects using colour blending determine how much fog colour to add to each pixel based on:

answers.A: the surface normal direction relative to the camera creating angular fog density variation
answers.B: a linear or exponential function of the distance between the pixel and the eye-point
answers.C: a 3D volumetric texture sampled along the ray from the camera to the surface point
answers.D: the object's material transparency value combined with a global fog density parameter

question: Which fog rendering technique can create non-uniform patchy fog AND allows the appearance to change as the eye-point moves?

answers.A: colour blending using distance-based weighting factors for each pixel
answers.B: texture mapping using pre-generated fog depth textures selected by distance
answers.C: volumetric rendering using a 3D volume applied between primitives and the eye
answers.D: procedural noise functions applied as a post-processing filter to the rendered image

question: A surface appears very bright at grazing angles but relatively dark when viewed head-on. Which phenomenon from the course material best explains this behaviour?

answers.A: subsurface scattering where light enters the material and exits at distant surface points
answers.B: the Fresnel effect where reflectivity increases as the viewing angle becomes more glancing
answers.C: anisotropic roughness where the surface microfacets are aligned in a preferred direction
answers.D: the Beer-Lambert law where light absorption increases with path length through the medium

question: An artist wants a material that shows a clear reflective layer over a coloured base, like car paint. In OpenPBR, the correct component to configure is:

answers.A: the Fuzz component with high weight to create grazing-angle glow over the base colour
answers.B: the Coat component representing a thin transparent specular layer on top of the base material
answers.C: the Thin Film component creating interference-based iridescent colours over the surface
answers.D: the Transmission component with a high IOR to create a glass-like layer above the pigment

question: A rendering needs to show light focused through a glass sphere onto a surface behind it. Standard ray tracing alone will NOT produce this effect because:

answers.A: ray tracing cannot simulate refraction through transparent objects with different indices of refraction
answers.B: ray tracing traces rays from the eye, so concentrated light patterns from the light source are not captured
answers.C: ray tracing does not support recursive ray spawning for both reflected and refracted ray paths
answers.D: ray tracing can only handle specular reflections and cannot compute diffuse light transport

question: Which of the following correctly orders the rendering techniques from fastest to slowest for a complex scene?

answers.A: direct illumination, ambient occlusion, ray tracing, path tracing with full GI
answers.B: path tracing with full GI, ray tracing, ambient occlusion, direct illumination
answers.C: ambient occlusion, direct illumination, path tracing with full GI, ray tracing
answers.D: ray tracing, path tracing with full GI, direct illumination, ambient occlusion

question: The RGBE format (Red-Green-Blue Exponent) developed by Greg Ward is used to store HDR images. Its advantage over standard 8-bit RGB is that it can:

answers.A: compress image data losslessly to reduce file sizes for network transmission
answers.B: store a wider range of luminance values using a shared exponent with less storage than full floating-point
answers.C: encode the full visible spectrum including ultraviolet and infrared wavelength data
answers.D: preserve alpha channel transparency information alongside high dynamic range colour

question: When creating an HDRI light probe using a regular camera, the photographer takes multiple exposures at different shutter speeds. The purpose of varying the exposure is to:

answers.A: capture different colour temperatures from the scene at each exposure level
answers.B: properly image both the darkest and brightest regions to solve for the full illumination range
answers.C: increase the spatial resolution by stitching together multiple images of the same field
answers.D: compensate for lens distortion that varies with the aperture setting of the camera

question: In the Phong model, the diffuse energy D = kd * id * cos(theta). An equivalent formulation using vectors replaces cos(theta) with:

answers.A: the cross product of the normal vector N and the light vector L
answers.B: the dot product of the normalised normal vector N and the normalised light vector L
answers.C: the dot product of the view vector V and the ideal reflection vector R
answers.D: the cross product of the view vector V and the surface tangent vector T

question: An object is rendered using only the Lambert reflection model. Compared to the full Phong model, the rendered object will appear:

answers.A: identical because Lambert and Phong produce the same ambient and diffuse components
answers.B: flat and without highlights because the Lambert model lacks specular reflection
answers.C: brighter overall because Lambert concentrates all light energy into the diffuse term
answers.D: darker overall because Lambert ignores the ambient component of the lighting equation

question: Which of the following is true about the relationship between transparency and translucency as described in the slides?

answers.A: Transparency follows Snell's Law of refraction while translucency does not necessarily follow it
answers.B: Translucency follows Snell's Law of refraction while transparency scatters light within the material
answers.C: Both transparency and translucency scatter light equally but differ only in the degree of scattering
answers.D: Both transparency and translucency follow Snell's Law but differ in their absorption coefficients

question: OpenPBR lists several organizations that collaborated on its development. Which of the following is NOT listed as a contributing organization?

answers.A: Adobe and its digital content creation division
answers.B: Autodesk and its rendering technology team
answers.C: Epic Games and its Unreal Engine development team
answers.D: Pixar and the Academy Software Foundation

question: In OpenPBR, subsurface scattering anisotropy set to +1 means light scatters primarily:

answers.A: backward toward the light source, typical of dense crystalline materials
answers.B: in all directions equally with no directional preference whatsoever
answers.C: forward through the material, typical of skin and biological tissue
answers.D: perpendicular to the surface normal, typical of thin flat materials

question: A desert scene uses displacement mapping with procedural textures to simulate approximately 1x10^12 polygons. The input geometry actually contains:

answers.A: approximately 30 million polygons with per-vertex height offsets pre-computed
answers.B: approximately 110 thousand polygons with the GPU displacing geometry from the procedural texture
answers.C: approximately 4,860 polygons using an adaptive tessellation scheme at render time
answers.D: approximately 20 triangles from a base icosahedron subdivided by the displacement map

question: The rendering equation cannot be solved exactly in finite time on a computer. Different GI renderers approach this by:

answers.A: solving the equation analytically for simple scenes and using lookup tables for complex ones
answers.B: finding approximate solutions that are reasonably close within a reasonable amount of time
answers.C: replacing the integral with a closed-form summation that converges to the exact answer
answers.D: splitting the equation into independent spectral components solved sequentially per wavelength

question: Which rendering system was first used by Pixar for path tracing in the film Monster's University?

answers.A: Arnold Renderer developed by Solid Angle for path-traced GI
answers.B: RenderMan which Pixar reworked to include path tracing capabilities
answers.C: Mental Ray developed by Mental Images with integrated path tracing
answers.D: V-Ray developed by Chaos Group with adaptive path tracing sampling

question: Network rendering stores rendered frames as raw uncompressed files (like TIFF or TARGA) for two specific reasons. Which pair of reasons is correct?

answers.A: compression takes valuable render time, and uncompressed files avoid compression artefacts
answers.B: uncompressed files are smaller than compressed formats, and they load faster into compositing software
answers.C: raw formats preserve alpha channels better, and they are compatible with more operating systems
answers.D: uncompressed files enable faster network transfer, and they require less disk space per frame

question: A scene has dynamic cloth simulation with inter-frame dependency. This scene cannot be rendered using:

answers.A: distributed rendering because it processes one frame at a time in sequential order
answers.B: network rendering because frames are sent to different machines without guaranteed order
answers.C: a single workstation because it lacks the processing power for cloth simulation calculations
answers.D: any global illumination method because cloth materials are incompatible with ray-based rendering
